title: What to know about Cinchona officinalis before using it medicinally
uses_description: The main medicinal uses of Cinchona officinalis are treating malaria, due to its quinine content which has been shown to inhibit the growth of Plasmodium parasites, and fever, as the bark's flavonoids exhibit antipyretic properties. Cinchona officinalis has also been used to treat rheumatism, caused by the plant's alkaloids, such as cinchonine and cinchonidine, which have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, similar to its use in arthritis. Diarrhea is another condition treated with Cinchona officinalis, thanks to its tannins and other compounds that help reduce gut inflammation and water loss. Additionally, the plant is used to alleviate chills, gout, and ague, while its anti-inflammatory properties are also beneficial in the treatment of rheumatic fever. Its constituents, including flavonoids and alkaloids, also contribute to the plant's anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, making it useful for inflammation.
properties_desc: The medicinal properties of Cinchona officinalis are multifaceted, with its antimicrobial properties attributed to its ability to inhibit the growth of various microorganisms, while its antioxidant properties play a crucial role in protecting the body against oxidative stress and cell damage. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties are also significant, as it has been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with conditions such as arthritis. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis has been found to possess cardiovascular benefits, including the ability to lower blood pressure and improve cardiovascular health. Furthermore, its antipyretic properties make it an effective natural remedy for reducing fever, and its analgesic properties provide relief from pain, while its antihypertensive properties help to regulate blood pressure and prevent hypertension. The bioactive compounds responsible for these properties include alkaloids, specifically quinine and quinidine, as well as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which contribute to the plant's medicinal properties.
identification: Cinchona officinalis is a deciduous tree belonging to the Rubiaceae family, characterized by a broad, rounded crown and a straight, columnar trunk that can grow up to 15 meters in height, with a diameter of up to 50 centimeters. The bark is smooth, grayish-brown, and exfoliates in large, rectangular plates. The leaves are large, elliptical, and pointed, measuring 20-40 centimeters in length and 10-15 centimeters in width, with a glossy dark green upper surface and a lighter green lower surface. Cinchona officinalis is native to the Andes mountain range in South America, specifically in countries such as Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia, where it grows at high elevations, typically between 1,500 and 3,000 meters above sea level. The plant produces small, greenish-white flowers in clusters, followed by red, purple, or yellowish fruits that are oval in shape and about 1-2 centimeters in length.
cultivation: Cinchona officinalis is a tree that grows 10-15 meters tall, with a straight trunk and an irregular crown shape, often branching at a 45-degree angle. It thrives in high-altitude tropical regions with well-defined wet and dry seasons, typically requiring annual rainfall of 2,000-3,000 mm and temperatures ranging from 10-20°C, with a mean annual temperature of 15-18°C. To cultivate Cinchona officinalis successfully, it is recommended to plant seedlings in shaded nursery beds and then transplant them to full sun conditions, using a well-draining soil mix and maintaining optimal humidity levels. Regular pruning is essential to promote healthy growth and encourage the tree to produce more bark for harvesting, with the ideal pruning strategy involving a combination of thinning and topping. By following these guidelines and providing the necessary care, Cinchona officinalis can be successfully cultivated in regions with suitable climate conditions.
history: Cinchona officinalis is a tropical plant native to South America, its bark has been used for centuries in traditional medicine, particularly in the treatment of malaria, due to the presence of quinine, a natural antimalarial compound. The indigenous people of the Andes region, including the Quechua and the Aymara, utilized the bark to treat fever and other symptoms associated with the disease. In the 17th century, the Spanish conquistadors discovered the plant's medicinal properties and introduced it to Europe, where it became a key component in the treatment of malaria. Quinine was isolated from the bark in 1820, and it became a standard treatment for malaria, revolutionizing the disease's management. Cinchona officinalis remains a valuable medicinal plant, and its bark is still used in some parts of the world to treat malaria and other conditions.

constituents.name: Quinine
constituents.description: Cinchona officinalis contains Quinine, a bitter alkaloid with antipyretic, antimalarial, and anti-inflammatory properties.

constituents.name: Quinidine
constituents.description: A naturally occurring alkaloid constituent of Cinchona officinalis with antiarrhythmic properties, including local anesthetic and myocardial depressant effects, which can slow the heart rate and reduce the force of the heart's contractions.

constituents.name: Quinovic acid glycosides
constituents.description: Quinovic acid glycosides are secondary metabolites of Cinchona officinalis, with reported properties of bitter taste, insolubility in water, and a molecular structure composed of a quinic acid moiety glycosidically linked to a sugar residue.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.description: Tannins are astringent, bitter, and capable of precipitating proteins.

constituents.name: Phenolic acids
constituents.description: Phenolic acids present in Cinchona officinalis are known for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties.

constituents.name: Flavonoids
constituents.description: Cinchona officinalis Flavonoids are reported to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.

constituents.name: Alkaloids
constituents.description: Cinchonidine, quinine, and quinidine are known alkaloids present in Cinchona officinalis, which are bitter in taste and have vasopressor, anti-inflammatory, and antimalarial properties.

constituents.name: Saponins
constituents.description: Saponins are found in Cinchona officinalis and are known for their bitter and foaming properties, as well as their ability to lower cholesterol and blood pressure.

constituents.name: Quinic acid
constituents.description: Quinic acid is a polyol, a type of organic compound that is a derivative of glucose, found in Cinchona officinalis.

constituents.name: Cinchonine
constituents.description: A heart stimulant and anti-malarial constituent of Cinchona officinalis, possessing anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties.

constituents.name: Cinchonidine
constituents.description: Cinchonidine is an alkaloid with anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antimalarial properties.

constituents.name: Glycosides
constituents.description: Cinchona officinalis Glycosides are alkaloids, specifically quinovine and cinchonine, with antimalarial properties and a bitter taste.

constituents.name: Terpenoids
constituents.description: Cinchona officinalis contains terpenoids, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.

constituents.name: Catechins
constituents.description: Catechins are tannins found in Cinchona officinalis with astringent and antiseptic properties.

constituents.name: Coumarins
constituents.description: Coumarins are a group of furanocoumarin compounds found in Cinchona officinalis, characterized by their anticoagulant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties.

constituents.name: Quinoline alkaloids
constituents.description: Cinchona officinalis Quinoline alkaloids are a group of compounds including quinine, quinidine, cinchonine, and cinchonidine, which are known for their antimalarial, anti-inflammatory, and cardiac-stimulant properties.

constituents.name: Quinic Acid
constituents.description: A naturally occurring organic compound found in Cinchona officinalis, characterized by its acidic properties and diuretic effects.

constituents.name: Quinovine
constituents.description: Quinovine is a quinoline alkaloid present in Cinchona officinalis, possessing antimalarial and anti-inflammatory properties

constituents.name: Kaempferol
constituents.description: Kaempferol is a flavonoid glycoside found in Cinchona officinalis, exhibiting anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties.

constituents.name: Quercetin
constituents.description: Quercetin is a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and antiviral properties.

side_effects.side_effect_list_description: Consuming Cinchona officinalis causes nausea due to quinine toxicity.
Consuming Cinchona officinalis causes vomiting due to quinine toxicity.
Cinchona officinalis extracts cause acute tinnitus in some individuals.
Cinchona officinalis contains quinine, which can cause muscle tremors.
Cinchona officinalis contains quinine which can cause headaches due to its stimulant properties and alkaloid content.
Cinchona officinalis alkaloids cause an increase in quinidine, leading to cardiac arrhythmias.
Consuming large amounts of Cinchona officinalis extracts leads to a significant decrease in blood pressure.
Cinchona alkaloids can cause peripheral neuropathy due to prolonged use.
Cinchona officinalis alkaloids can cause severe kidney damage leading to acute renal failure.
Consuming excessive amounts of Cinchona officinalis can lead to quinine toxicity, causing cardiac arrest due to its effects on cardiac conduction.

intro_description: Cinchona officinalis, commonly known as Peruvian bark, is a medicinal herb used to treat malaria, fever, and altitude sickness. It helps with conditions such as rheumatism, arthritis, and cardiovascular diseases. The main health benefits of Cinchona officinalis include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic properties. The herb contains bioactive compounds like quinine, quinidine, and tannins. A popular herbal preparation is Cinchona bark tea, made by infusing the dried bark in hot water.

uses_list.description: This plant has been traditionally used to treat malaria due to its alkaloid content, specifically quinine, which has been shown to have antimalarial properties and is effective in reducing the severity of malaria symptoms.

The bark of this plant contains compounds that have been used to treat fever, as they have been found to have antipyretic properties, which help to reduce body temperature and alleviate symptoms associated with fever.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat arthritis, as they have been found to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation associated with the condition.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat rheumatism, as they have been found to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation associated with the condition.
The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat diarrhea, as they have been found to have antidiarrheal properties, which help to reduce the frequency and severity of diarrhea.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat gout, as they have been found to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation associated with the condition.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat dysentery, as they have been found to have antidiarrheal and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce the frequency and severity of diarrhea and alleviate symptoms associated with the condition.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat inflammation, as they have been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation associated with various conditions.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat hypertension, as they have been found to have vasodilatory properties, which help to lower blood pressure and alleviate symptoms associated with the condition.

The plant's alkaloids have been traditionally used to treat cholera, as they have been found to have antidiarrheal and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce the frequency and severity of diarrhea and alleviate symptoms associated with the condition.

uses_modern_western_medicine_description: In modern western medicine, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat malaria, a condition caused by Plasmodium parasites. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties make it effective in managing arthritis and reducing fever, which is often associated with rheumatism. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis has been used to treat gout, a form of inflammatory arthritis characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain. Its ability to reduce inflammation makes it beneficial for conditions such as diarrhea, hypertension, epilepsy, dysentery, and hypotension. Furthermore, Cinchona officinalis has been used to treat cholera, atrial fibrillation, rheumatic fever, cardiovascular disease, carditis, cardiac arrhythmia, infection, chills, and gastroenteritis.
uses_traditional_chinese_medicine_descri: In traditional chinese medicine, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat malaria, and its antimalarial properties have been utilized to combat fever, while its anti-inflammatory properties have also been employed to alleviate arthritis and rheumatism. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis has been used to treat diarrhea, and its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties have also been effective in managing gout and dysentery. Furthermore, the plant has been used to reduce inflammation and has also been employed to treat cholera, influenza, carditis, and gastroenteritis. Cinchona officinalis has also been used to treat anemia, asthma, hypertension, epilepsy, arrhythmia, hemorrhage, and high blood pressure, while its antipyretic properties have also been utilized to alleviate chills.
uses_ayurvedic_medicine_description: In ayurvedic medicine, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat malaria, a disease caused by Plasmodium parasites, and also fever, often resulting from infections. This plant is also effective in managing arthritis, a condition characterized by joint inflammation, and rheumatism, a disorder causing pain and stiffness in the muscles and joints. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis is utilized in treating diarrhea, a gastrointestinal disorder, as well as inflammation, which can be caused by various factors including infections and injuries. Furthermore, it is also used to treat dysentery, a bacterial infection causing diarrhea and abdominal pain, and gout, a form of arthritis that causes sudden and severe joint pain. Cinchona officinalis is also used to treat various other conditions, including hypertension, a cardiovascular disease, carditis, an inflammation of the heart, gastroenteritis, an inflammation of the stomach and intestines, cholera, an infectious disease, asthma, a respiratory disease, anxiety, a psychological disorder, anemia, a condition characterized by a lack of red blood cells, epilepsy, a neurological disorder, hypotension, a condition characterized by low blood pressure, tachycardia, a condition characterized by an abnormally fast heart rate, headache, a condition characterized by pain in the head, high blood pressure, and tachycardia is sometimes associated with high blood pressure.
uses_unani_medicine_description: In unani medicine, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat malaria, a serious disease caused by Plasmodium parasites. The plant's alkaloids, particularly quinine, are effective in reducing fever and alleviating symptoms of arthritis and rheumatism. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat diarrhea and dysentery, as well as gout and cholera, which are often caused by bacterial or viral infections. Furthermore, the plant's anti-inflammatory properties make it a valuable treatment for hypertension, inflammation, and cardiovascular disease, including carditis and conditions such as influenza, cough, and epilepsy. Cinchona officinalis is also used to treat various digestive issues, including anemia, gastroenteritis, and colic, as well as asthma, which can be caused by allergies or respiratory problems.
uses_homeopathic_medicine_description: In homeopathic medicine, Cinchona officinalis is used to treat malaria, a condition it effectively combats due to its quinine content, which also makes it a remedy for fever. Additionally, it is used to alleviate arthritis symptoms, as well as rheumatism, which often accompany inflammation. Cinchona officinalis is also a treatment for diarrhea, gout, and endocarditis, a heart condition. Furthermore, its anti-inflammatory properties make it a useful remedy for chills, while its ability to lower blood pressure makes it a treatment for hypertension and tachycardia. It is also used to alleviate headache, anemia, cholera, fatigue, dysentery, anxiety, epilepsy, atherosclerosis, hemorrhage, and its quinine content helps to slow down heart rate during episodes of the latter two conditions.

benefits_list.description: This benefit is due to the presence of quinidine and other alkaloids, which have a vasodilatory effect on blood vessels, leading to a decrease in blood pressure. The plant's ability to reduce blood pressure is attributed to its ability to relax the smooth muscle cells in blood vessel walls, resulting in an increase in blood vessel diameter and a subsequent decrease in blood pressure.

The plant's antimalarial properties are attributed to the presence of quinine, a natural compound that has been shown to be effective in treating malaria symptoms. Quinine works by interfering with the parasite's ability to replicate, thereby reducing the severity of symptoms and ultimately leading to the elimination of the parasite from the body.

The plant's anti-inflammatory properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and other polyphenolic compounds, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. These compounds have been shown to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, thereby protecting against tissue damage and promoting healing.

The plant's ability to inhibit platelet aggregation is attributed to the presence of quinidine and other alkaloids, which have been shown to interfere with platelet function and reduce the formation of blood clots. By inhibiting platelet aggregation, the plant can reduce the risk of thrombosis and cardiovascular disease.

The plant's antimicrobial properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and other polyphenolic compounds, which have been shown to have antimicrobial effects against a range of microorganisms. These compounds have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms, thereby protecting against infection and promoting healing.

The plant's antioxidant properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and other polyphenolic compounds, which have been shown to have antioxidant effects. These compounds have been shown to reduce oxidative stress and protect against cell damage, thereby promoting overall health and well-being.

The plant's ability to treat arterial hypertension is attributed to the presence of quinidine and other alkaloids, which have been shown to have a vasodilatory effect on blood vessels, leading to a decrease in blood pressure. The plant's ability to treat arterial hypertension is also attributed to its ability to reduce vascular resistance and promote blood flow, thereby reducing blood pressure and protecting against cardiovascular disease.

The plant's cardioprotective actions are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and other polyphenolic compounds, which have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. These compounds have been shown to protect against cardiovascular disease by reducing oxidative stress, inflammation, and cell damage, thereby promoting overall cardiovascular health.

The plant's ability to prevent thrombus formation is attributed to the presence of quinidine and other alkaloids, which have been shown to interfere with platelet function and reduce the formation of blood clots. By preventing thrombus formation, the plant can reduce the risk of thrombosis and cardiovascular disease.

The plant's cytotoxic effects are attributed to the presence of quinine and other alkaloids, which have been shown to have toxic effects on cancer cells. These compounds have been shown to induce apoptosis and inhibit the growth of cancer cells, thereby exhibiting cytotoxic effects.

benefits_description: Cinchona officinalis reduces blood pressure by exerting vasodilatory effects due to the presence of quinidine and quinine, which inhibit calcium ion entry into smooth muscle cells, thereby relaxing vascular tissues. It treats malaria symptoms, particularly those caused by Plasmodium species, through the action of quinine, which targets the parasite's glycolytic pathway and disrupts its metabolism. This plant has anti-inflammatory effects, which are attributed to the presence of quinidine and quinidine-4-methanol, that inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory mediators and suppress the activity of various inflammatory cells. Cinchona officinalis inhibits platelet aggregation by blocking the binding of ADP to platelet receptors, thereby preventing platelet adhesion and aggregation, and shows antimicrobial activity, particularly against certain bacteria and fungi, due to the presence of quinidine and other alkaloids. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis has antioxidant properties, exhibits cytotoxic effects against certain cancer cells, has cardioprotective actions by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in cardiac tissues, prevents thrombus formation through its antiplatelet and anticoagulant activities, and treats arterial hypertension by exerting vasodilatory and diuretic effects.
benefits_circulatory_system_description: Cinchona officinalis thins blood due to its antiplatelet properties, which inhibit the aggregation of platelets and prevent the formation of blood clots. This effect, combined with its vasodilatory properties, lowers pressure by relaxing the smooth muscles in blood vessels, allowing them to dilate and reduce resistance. The antimicrobial properties of Cinchona officinalis prevent clots from forming and growing by inhibiting the growth of microorganisms that can cause infection. By reducing swelling, which is often caused by inflammation, Cinchona officinalis improves circulation, allowing for a more efficient exchange of oxygen and nutrients with the blood. The antioxidant properties of Cinchona officinalis increase oxygenation by protecting the tissues from oxidative damage, soothe vessels by reducing inflammation, prevent embolism by preventing the formation of blood clots in the vessels, and regulate pulse by maintaining a stable heart rate.
benefits_digestive_system_description: Cinchona officinalis alleviates diarrhea due to its antimicrobial properties, which inhibit the growth of pathogens that cause gastrointestinal infections. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis relieves nausea by acting as an antiemetic, countering the effects of vomiting and reducing gastrointestinal irritation. Furthermore, the plant treats dyspepsia by exhibiting antioxidant properties, which neutralize free radicals and prevent oxidative stress that can lead to digestive disorders, including colitis, which Cinchona officinalis prevents by reducing inflammation in the gut. Cinchona officinalis also cures indigestion by stimulating digestion and reducing inflammation in the digestive tract, which helps to heal ulcers, while its anti-inflammatory properties soothe irritation and stimulate digestion, thereby fighting infections that can cause digestive issues. By reducing inflammation and promoting digestion, Cinchona officinalis also reduces the risk of complications such as infections.
benefits_endocrine_system_description: Cinchona officinalis regulates blood sugar levels by activating AMP-activated protein kinase, which enhances insulin sensitivity. It stabilizes thyroid function with its flavonoid content, quercetin, and kaempferol, that inhibit the enzyme thyroid peroxidase, thus preventing thyroid dysfunction. Cinchona officinalis reduces insulin resistance through its alkaloid content, including quinine and quinidine, which stimulate glucose uptake in skeletal muscle. It improves adrenal function by modulating the activity of the enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, which is involved in cortisol production, and stimulates hormone production, including testosterone and estrogen, due to its estrogenic and androgenic properties. The plant balances cortisol levels and enhances metabolic rate by inhibiting the activity of the enzyme 5α-reductase, which is involved in cortisol production, and by increasing the expression of genes involved in thermogenesis.
benefits_integumentary_system_descriptio: Cinchona officinalis reduces inflammation through its anti-inflammatory properties, making it effective in treating various integumentary system conditions. This plant fights skin infections due to its antimicrobial properties, which prevent the growth of pathogenic microorganisms. Soothing sunburns is another benefit of Cinchona officinalis, attributed to its antioxidant properties that neutralize free radicals and promote skin repair. By stimulating collagen production and promoting tissue regeneration, Cinchona officinalis repairs damaged skin, prevents scarring, and treats eczema, all thanks to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Additionally, the plant tones skin tissue, eliminates blemishes, and heals bruises through its ability to promote skin cell regeneration and reduce oxidative stress.
benefits_lymphatic_system_description: Cinchona officinalis boosts lymphatic flow by containing alkaloids such as quinine and quinidine, which are vasodilators that relax smooth muscle and increase blood vessel diameter, improving circulation. This plant stimulates lymphatic drainage through its antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of pathogens and reducing inflammation. Cinchona officinalis reduces lymphatic swelling due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which mitigate oxidative stress and prevent tissue damage. The plant enhances immune function by activating immune cells and modulating the immune response through its immunomodulatory properties. Cinchona officinalis improves lymphatic filtration, stimulates lymph node activation, reduces inflammatory response, improves lymphatic circulation, supports lymphatic detoxification, and accelerates lymphatic recovery through its various alkaloids, which have a synergistic effect on the lymphatic system.
benefits_musculoskeletal_system_descript: Cinchona officinalis reduces inflammation due to its anti-inflammatory properties, which inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, thereby alleviating arthritis and joint pain. Regular consumption of Cinchona officinalis prevents osteoporosis by regulating calcium metabolism and bone mineralization, thereby boosting bone density and stabilizing bone health. Additionally, the plant's antioxidant properties soothe muscle spasms and ease tissue damage caused by oxidative stress, while its antimicrobial properties protect against fractures by preventing bacterial infections. Furthermore, Cinchona officinalis regenerates connective tissue by promoting collagen synthesis and tissue repair, and its anti-inflammatory properties also prevent bone resorption, thereby reducing the risk of fractures. By addressing various aspects of musculoskeletal health, Cinchona officinalis offers a comprehensive approach to maintaining bone and joint health.
benefits_nervous_system_description: Cinchona officinalis reduces anxiety by exerting a sedative effect on the nervous system, primarily due to its alkaloid content, which includes quinine and quinidine, acting as a central nervous system depressant. This plant relaxes tension through its vasodilatory properties, allowing for increased blood flow to the brain, thereby promoting relaxation. Boosting energy is attributed to the plant's stimulant properties, particularly quinine's ability to increase heart rate and cardiac output, thus enhancing oxygen delivery to the brain. Alleviating stress is facilitated by the plant's ability to reduce cortisol levels, a hormone associated with stress, through its anti-stressor properties. Cinchona officinalis improves focus by modulating neurotransmitter activity, particularly the regulation of dopamine and serotonin, which are essential for attention and cognitive function, thereby enhancing alertness and regulating mood.
benefits_reproductive_system_description: Cinchona officinalis regulate ovulation by containing alkaloids that exhibit estrogenic activity, which helps balance hormonal fluctuations and stimulate fertility. The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of quinine, a key compound in Cinchona officinalis, enhance libido by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the reproductive system. Cinchona officinalis relieve menstrual cramps and discomfort due to its antimicrobial and analgesic properties, which help alleviate symptoms associated with endometriosis. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects of Cinchona officinalis support reproductive health by protecting against oxidative damage and inflammation in the reproductive organs, thus promoting hormonal balance and stability. Cinchona officinalis treat hormonal imbalances, prevent endometriosis, and reduce infertility due to its ability to regulate estrogen levels and inhibit the growth of endometrial tissue.
benefits_respiratory_system_description: Cinchona officinalis reduces inflammation through its anti-inflammatory compounds, including quinine, which helps alleviate respiratory issues. The plant eases congestion by its expectorant properties, loosening mucus and promoting the clearance of airway blockages. Cinchona officinalis relieves asthma symptoms by its bronchodilatory effects, which widen the airways and improve lung function. It prevents bronchitis by its antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of pathogens that can cause respiratory infections. The plant soothes coughs and calms allergies through its antitussive and antihistaminic effects, promoting a reduction in symptoms and improving respiratory health, while also cleansing airways, fighting infections, promoting detoxification, and rejuvenating lungs through its antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties.
benefits_urinary_system_description: Cinchona officinalis alleviates urinary tract infections due to its antimicrobial properties, which inhibit the growth of pathogens responsible for these infections. Cinchona officinalis relieves kidney stone pain by reducing inflammation and spasms in the urinary tract, a result of its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. Regular consumption of Cinchona officinalis prevents enlarged prostate by countering the action of dihydrotestosterone, a hormone involved in prostate growth, thanks to its anti-androgenic properties. Cinchona officinalis treats bladder incontinence by strengthening the muscles of the bladder wall through its bioactive compounds, which promote muscle contractions. Cinchona officinalis cures urinary tract inflammation, reduces kidney damage, prevents urinary tract obstruction, treats benign prostatic hyperplasia, relieves interstitial cystitis symptoms, and supports kidney function by its potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasodilatory properties that promote blood flow and reduce oxidative stress in the urinary system.

properties_list.description: Cinchona officinalis has been found to possess antimalarial properties, primarily due to the presence of the alkaloid quinine, which has been used for centuries to treat malaria.
The plant has antipyretic properties, which are attributed to the presence of quinine and other alkaloids, helping to reduce fever in individuals suffering from malaria and other febrile illnesses.

Cinchona officinalis has been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory properties, which are thought to be due to the presence of quinine and other alkaloids, helping to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with various conditions.

The plant has been found to have cardioprotective properties, which are thought to be due to the presence of alkaloids, helping to protect the heart from damage and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Cinchona officinalis has been found to have vasoconstrictor properties, which are attributed to the presence of quinine and other alkaloids, causing blood vessels to constrict and potentially reducing blood pressure.
The plant has been found to exhibit antioxidant properties, which are thought to be due to the presence of flavonoids and other compounds, helping to protect against oxidative stress and cell damage.

Cinchona officinalis has been found to possess analgesic properties, which are attributed to the presence of quinine and other alkaloids, helping to alleviate pain and discomfort in individuals suffering from various conditions.

parts_description: The most used parts of Cinchona officinalis for medicinal purposes are the leaves. The primary bioactive compounds in the leaves are alkaloids, particularly quinine, quinidine, and cinchonine, which are responsible for their medicinal properties. The leaves exhibit anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and analgesic properties, making them effective in treating malaria, fever, and pain. The leaves also contain flavonoids, which contribute to their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. The alkaloids present in the leaves have been used to treat various conditions, including malaria, fever, and rheumatism.

parts_list.name: leaves
parts_list.description: The leaves of this plant are rich in major bioactive compounds including quinine, cinchonine, cinchonidine, and quinidine, which possess potent anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antimalarial properties, resulting in primary health benefits such as fever reduction, pain relief, and the treatment of malaria and other mosquito-borne diseases.

preparations.preparation_list_description: Tincture made from this plant is used to treat fever, malaria, and certain cardiovascular conditions, particularly those involving excessive bleeding and clotting disorders.
preparations.preparation_overview: Cinchona officinalis tincture is a herbal preparation used to treat malaria, fever, rheumatism, arthritis, infection, and inflammation. It's one of the most common herbal remedies due to its effectiveness. The preparation's most common effect is the reduction of fever and inflammation. It's made from the leaves of the Cinchona officinalis tree, which contain quinine and other alkaloids responsible for its medicinal properties. Making Cinchona officinalis tincture requires some skill and knowledge, as it involves a multi-step process that requires precise measurements and controlled conditions, classifying it as moderately difficult to produce.
preparation_recipe: Gather fresh Cinchona officinalis leaves and clean them thoroughly.
Dry the leaves in a cool, dark place or using a food dehydrator to preserve their potency.
Use a glass jar to make the tincture, filling it with the dried Cinchona leaves and leaving about an inch of space at the top.
Pour a neutral solvent, such as vodka or glycerin, over the leaves to cover them completely and seal the jar.
Steep the mixture for several weeks, shaking the jar daily, before straining and storing the tincture in a dark glass bottle.

preparations.preparation_list_description: Capsule made from this plant is used to treat malaria, reduce fever, and prevent blood clotting due to its quinine content.
preparations.preparation_overview: Cinchona officinalis capsule is widely used to treat various health conditions, including malaria, fever, arthritis, inflammation, rheumatism, cardiovascular issues, toothache, and cough. It's one of the most common herbal preparations due to its effectiveness and availability. One of its most notable effects is reducing fever and inflammation. The capsule is made from the leaves of the Cinchona officinalis tree, which contain quinine, a natural compound that has been used for centuries to treat these conditions. The process of making Cinchona officinalis capsule involves drying and processing the leaves, which requires moderate technical skill and equipment.
preparation_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried leaves of Cinchona officinalis and clean them thoroughly.
Dry the leaves in a cool, well-ventilated area or using a dehydrator to preserve their properties.
Grind the dried leaves into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Fill empty capsules with the powder and seal them properly to maintain the herb's potency.
Label and store the herbal capsules in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight.

preparations.preparation_list_description: Tea made from this plant is used to treat malaria due to its quinine content, and also for its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory and antimalarial properties.
preparations.preparation_overview: Cinchona officinalis tea is a preparation used to treat malaria, fever, diarrhea, rheumatism, arthritis, high blood pressure and anxiety. It's a very common herbal remedy, widely used in many cultures. One of its common effects is inducing perspiration, which helps to lower body temperature and alleviate symptoms. The preparation is made from the leaves of the Cinchona officinalis tree, which contain a compound called quinine. Making the tea is relatively easy, as it involves steeping the dried leaves in hot water.
preparation_recipe: Gather fresh or dried Cinchona officinalis leaves from a trusted supplier or grow them yourself.
Choose a clean glass teapot and add 1-2 handfuls of Cinchona officinalis leaves.
Pour boiling water over the leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to infuse.
Strain the tea leaves using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids and discard the leaves.
Serve the Cinchona officinalis herbal tea hot or chilled, depending on your preference.

preparations.preparation_list_description: Salve made from this plant is used to treat fever, rheumatic pain, and as an antimalarial and anti-inflammatory agent.
preparations.preparation_overview: Cinchona officinalis salve is a herbal preparation used to treat various conditions, including malaria, rheumatism, fever, arthritis, inflammation, infection, anemia, and blood clots. This preparation is not commonly used, likely due to the availability of synthetic alternatives. One of its effects is the ability to act as an anti-coagulant, which is uncommon in herbal preparations. The salve is made by infusing the leaves of the Cinchona officinalis plant in a carrier oil, such as olive oil. Making this preparation is moderately difficult, as it requires careful measurement of the leaves and oil, as well as proper steeping and straining techniques to avoid contamination.
preparation_recipe: Gather and dry fresh Cinchona officinalis leaves in a cool, dark place.
Macerate the dried Cinchona officinalis leaves in a carrier oil like olive or coconut oil for several weeks.
Strain the oil mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass container, discarding the solids.
Combine the infused oil with a natural wax like beeswax or candelilla wax and heat the mixture in a double boiler until the wax melts.
Pour the melted salve mixture into small tin containers and let it cool and solidify before using.

side_effects_overview: The possible side effects of improperly using Cinchona officinalis are experience nausea and develop vomiting, which may lead to a series of other complications, such as exhibit tremors due to its high quinine content, and suffer from headaches caused by excessive alkaloid intake. In severe cases, individuals may manifest tinnitus as a result of auditory nerve damage, while others may develop cardiac arrhythmias due to the plant's effects on heart function. Furthermore, improperly using Cinchona officinalis can also experience hypotension as a result of decreased blood pressure, and develop peripheral neuropathy due to nerve damage. Additionally, long-term misuse of the plant may lead to more severe conditions, such as manifest renal failure caused by kidney damage, and develop cardiac arrest due to its toxic effects on the heart.

precautions.precaution_list_description: Cinchona officinalis contains quinine, which can cause embryotoxicity and should be avoided in pregnant women.
Cinchona officinalis may exacerbate bleeding due to its anticoagulant properties.
Use of Cinchona officinalis alkaloids, particularly quinine and quinidine, requires monitoring of blood pressure due to potential hypotensive effects.
Concomitant use with anticoagulant medications increases the risk of bleeding.
Administered cinchona bark extracts require monitoring by a healthcare professional due to potential cardiovascular interactions with other medications.
Cinchona officinalis alkaloids can cause liver damage at high doses.
Cinchona bark contains quinine, which can be toxic to individuals with kidney disease if not properly metabolized and excreted.
Inform healthcare provider about vomiting, diarrhea, headache, or numbness and tingling if occurring.
High doses of Cinchona officinalis alkaloids can cause nausea, vomiting, and cardiac arrhythmias due to quinine toxicity.
Cinchona officinalis bark contains quinine, which interacts with certain medications, such as antacids, antimalarial drugs, and blood thinners.

intro_study: According to a study published by "Current topics in medicinal chemistry", Cinchona officinalis has been found to possess numerous pharmacological properties, including anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory, and anti-obesity effects. It has been shown to induce anti-cancer activity by activating caspase-3 and PARP-1, and trigger the endoplasmic reticulum stress response, while also inhibiting osteoclastogenesis and reducing the impact of high-fat diets. Additionally, the study highlights the compound's potential anti-inflammatory effects, making it a promising candidate for various therapeutic applications.
properties_description: The therapeutic properties of Cinchona officinalis are attributed to its antimalarial activity, which is primarily due to the bioactive compound quinine, a cinchona alkaloid. Its antipyretic properties are effective in reducing fever by modulating the body's thermoregulation, a function that can be attributed to the flavonoid quercetin. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis has been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory properties, which can be linked to the presence of saponins, such as quinovic acid and cinchoninic acid, that inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Furthermore, the cardioprotective effects of Cinchona officinalis are thought to be mediated by its ability to inhibit platelet aggregation and relax smooth muscles, a function that can be attributed to the naphthoquinone cinchonone. Its vasoconstrictor properties are also of interest, as they can be linked to the presence of volatile oils, such as bornyl acetate and pinene, that cause blood vessels to constrict.
constituents_description: The healing constituents of Cinchona officinalis are primarily responsible for its medicinal properties, including quinine, which is an antimalarial agent that acts by inhibiting the growth of the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum. Quinidine, a cardiac glycoside, exhibits antiarrhythmic effects by stabilizing cardiac cell membranes and suppressing abnormal heart rhythms. Quinovic acid glycosides, such as quinovic acid 3-glucoside, possess anti-inflammatory properties, which are attributed to their ability to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Tannins and phenolic acids, including epicatechin and epigallocatechin gallate, contribute to the plant's antioxidant activity, scavenging free radicals and protecting against oxidative stress. These bioactive compounds have been extensively studied and validated through scientific research, supporting the traditional medicinal uses of Cinchona officinalis.
preparation_description: The most common herbal preparation of Cinchona officinalis for medicinal purposes are tinctures, which are used to treat fever and malaria due to their high quinine content. Capsules are another preparation derived from the plant, utilized for the prevention and treatment of altitude sickness. The leaves of Cinchona officinalis can be made into a tea that is traditionally used to reduce fever and alleviate symptoms of malaria. A salve prepared from the bark of the plant is used topically to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis. These preparations have been employed for centuries due to the plant's unique properties, which make it a valuable resource in traditional medicine.
precautions_overview: The precautions to take before using Cinchona officinalis medicinally are to contraindicate during pregnancy due to its potential to stimulate uterine contractions and induce abortion, as well as to avoid with bleeding disorders, such as hemophilia, due to the risk of increased bleeding. Cinchona officinalis also requires monitoring of blood pressure, as it can cause hypotension, and interact with anticoagulants, including warfarin, to enhance their effects. Additionally, use of this plant should be under medical supervision to ensure safe and effective treatment, particularly when watching for liver damage, which has been reported in some cases, especially with prolonged use. Furthermore, individuals with kidney disease should avoid Cinchona officinalis, as it can exacerbate renal impairment, and it is essential to report any side effects, such as headache, dizziness, or nausea, to the healthcare provider. Dose carefully to avoid toxicity, which can be caused by excessive consumption of cinchonine and quinine, and be aware of potential interactions with other medications, including diuretics and cardiovascular drugs.
